title: FFmpeg fixes PixelSmash flaw in widely used video decoder
blurb: FFmpeg patched PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461), a high-severity heap out-of-bounds write in its MagicYUV decoder that JFrog used to achieve remote code execution against a Jellyfin media server through its normal library scan. Any application built on libavcodec is exposed, including Kodi, OBS Studio, Nextcloud, and desktop thumbnail generators, since a malicious AVI, MKV, or MOV file can trigger the flaw on open or preview.

title: Quadrupling code performance with a "useless" if
blurb: Adding a seemingly useless `if` branch to a pointer-chasing loop quadrupled its throughput by breaking a serial dependency chain the CPU couldn't parallelize. The author shows that the `j = next_j[i][j]` lookup forced each iteration to wait on the previous one, and a branch that lets the processor speculate past the load restores instruction-level parallelism.

title: Old and new apps, via modern coding agents
blurb: Terrence Tao used an AI coding agent to port two dozen of his defunct 1999 Java math applets to JavaScript in a matter of hours, restoring visualizations like a honeycomb tool that were previously non-functional. He found only one minor bug in the ported code, while the agent surfaced two bugs in his original work, and he also used it to finally build a special-relativity visualization tool he had abandoned decades ago.

title: Don't you mean extinct?
blurb: In 1993, Jurassic Park's photorealistic CGI dinosaurs blindsided stop-motion master Phil Tippett, who told Spielberg he felt "extinct" as decades of go-motion expertise became obsolete overnight. The author uses the anecdote to reframe programmer anxiety over LLMs: every generation faces a similar reinvention, from the early web to mobile-first, and the way to avoid extinction is to learn the new tool rather than resist it.

title: Since Chromium 148, Math.tanh is now fingerprintable to link underlying OS
blurb: Since Chromium 148, V8 computes Math.tanh through the host system's math library instead of its bundled fdlibm port, so the last bits of the result now vary by operating system. Because glibc, Apple's libsystem_m, and Windows UCRT round transcendental functions differently, a single call like Math.tanh(0.8) betrays the real OS, letting anti-bot systems catch a browser whose math contradicts its User-Agent.

title: European defencetech leader Helsing secures $1.8B Series E at $18B valuation
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B in a Series E at an $18B valuation, with new and existing investors including Lightspeed, Iconiq, and CPP Investments after demand exceeded the allocation. Founded in 2021, the European defence AI company builds software that fuses drone, radar, and satellite data into a real-time operational picture and has expanded into strike drones and underwater surveillance systems.

title: Show HN: Ant – A JavaScript runtime and ecosystem
blurb: Ant is a new JavaScript runtime and ecosystem introduced on Hacker News as an alternative to Node, Deno, and Bun. The project bundles a runtime with its own package management and standard library, aiming to reduce the toolchain sprawl that ships with typical JavaScript projects.

title: UPI: Anatomy of a Payment Transaction
blurb: UPI processed more than 2,272 crore payments in June 2026, more than any real-time payment system in the world, and this piece traces one transaction through the seven separate parties that clear it between the QR scan and the green tick. Each layer checks a single thing before handing off, and the article details where each can fail, plus how PhonePe and Google Pay came to carry roughly four-fifths of all volume.

title: Your code is fast if you're lucky
blurb: Modern compilers like Clang can turn a comparison into a fast, branch-free instruction, but only if your code is written in the style the optimizer recognizes. Working on a branchless quicksort built from sorting networks, the author found that trivial rewrites of the same comparison logic swing performance dramatically, since the compiler emits conditional moves in one form and slow branches in another.

title: Unauthenticated RCE in Motorola's MR2600 Router
blurb: A researcher found an unauthenticated RCE in Motorola's MR2600 router, where a botched SEAMA magic-byte check lets attackers upload malicious firmware unauthenticated.

title: Europe's Anduril rival Helsing raises $1.8 billion at $18 billion valuation
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation, making the Munich-based defense startup one of Europe's most valuable, with JPMorgan, Lightspeed, and Iconiq among the backers. The company builds AI-driven drones and underwater surveillance systems, including the HX-2 drones supplied to Ukraine, and positions itself as Europe's answer to Anduril, which raised $5B at a $61B valuation in May.

title: Nvidia, CoreWeave, and Nebius: Inside the Circular Financing of the GPU Boom
blurb: Nvidia-backed neoclouds CoreWeave and Nebius have each secured 3.5 GW of contracted power and soaring backlogs, but this analysis argues their buildouts run on GPU-backed debt and circular financing rather than profits. Nvidia's own investments and financial backstopping prop up customers who then buy its chips, a loop the author flags as the key risk to the durability of the GPU boom.

title: ZeroFS vs. Amazon S3 Files
blurb: ZeroFS and Amazon S3 Files both expose POSIX filesystems backed by object storage, but they take opposite approaches to bucket layout. S3 Files, built on Amazon EFS, keeps a one-to-one mapping where each mounted file becomes a normal S3 object readable by existing tools, while ZeroFS packs compressed, encrypted extents into opaque segments that trade direct S3 access for smaller storage and client-side encryption.

title: Munich-based defense tech startup Helsing raised a $1.8B Series E from Dragoneer, Iconiq, and others at an $18B valuation; Germany made a €270M order this year (Mark Bergen/Bloomberg)
blurb: Helsing raised a $1.8B Series E at an $18B valuation, led by investors including Dragoneer and Iconiq, making the Munich-based startup one of Europe's most valuable defense tech firms. Germany placed a €270M order with the company this year, underscoring how rising European defense budgets are pulling AI-focused military suppliers into the mainstream.

title: Claude Code sends 33k tokens before reading the prompt; OpenCode sends 7k
blurb: Claude Code sends about 33k tokens of system prompt, tool schemas, and injected scaffolding before a user prompt even arrives, while OpenCode sends roughly 7k on the same model and machine, according to measurements at the API boundary. OpenCode also caches a byte-identical request prefix once per session, whereas Claude Code rewrites premium-billed cache tokens mid-session, though its batching of tool calls can still win multi-step tasks on total spend.

title: Helsing raises $1.8bn backed by Goldman and Lightspeed
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation in a Series E backed by Goldman Sachs Alternatives, Lightspeed, JPMorganChase, Dragoneer, and Iconiq, marking one of the largest funding rounds ever for a European startup. The Munich defence firm, which builds battlefield AI, autonomous strike drones, and underwater surveillance gliders, will use the capital to expand AI platforms for European and allied armed forces amid rising defence budgets.

title: Unexpected Solidlike Fracture in Simple Liquids
blurb: Drexel University researchers found that a simple, nonelastic viscous fluid can fracture like glass instead of stretching, an effect previously thought impossible for liquids with almost no elasticity. Using extensional rheology and high-speed cameras, the team identified the snap as a brittle fracture, showing that even ordinary fluids like crude oil blends can crack under stress rather than flow.

title: FCC approves test of space mirror to light night sky
blurb: The FCC approved Reflect Orbital to test Earendil-1, a satellite that reflects sunlight back to Earth to light a five-kilometer-wide area on demand, over objections from astronomers citing safety and light-pollution risks. The company plans a constellation of more than 50,000 mirrors by 2035, while the FCC noted that harms to health, telescopes, and star-tracking cameras fall outside its spectrum-authorization remit.

title: China’s answer to SpaceX’s reusable rockets literally catches boosters in a net
blurb: China launched the Long March 10B on July 10 and recovered its first-stage booster by catching it in a cable net aboard a recovery ship, the Linghangzhe, marking the first time any program has captured an orbital-class booster this way. The medium-lift rocket carries roughly 16 metric tons to low-Earth orbit, and China plans to refly the recovered stage before the end of 2026 as it works to close SpaceX's launch-cadence lead.

title: 챗GPT 워크 출시의 의미…’비용 효율 경쟁’ 시대로 재편되는 기업용 AI 판도
blurb: OpenAI released ChatGPT Work, an enterprise agent platform that pairs ChatGPT, Codex, and the newly general-available GPT-5.6 to automate multi-step knowledge tasks across connected business apps under enterprise governance. GPT-5.6 ships in three tiers priced on performance per dollar, with the flagship Sol at $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens, signaling that inference cost, not benchmark scores, now drives enterprise AI buying.

title: Apple Sues OpenAI Over AI Talent Fight
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI, alleging former Apple employees improperly carried trade secrets to the startup as it expands its hardware and device ambitions. The suit reflects an intensifying fight for AI talent and hardware leadership, positioning the two companies as direct rivals rather than the model-supply partners they once appeared to be.

title: Helsing raises $1.8B from Dragoneer and Lightspeed to scale its AI defence platform
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B in a Series E at an $18B valuation, co-led by Dragoneer and Lightspeed, with Goldman Sachs Alternatives, JPMorgan, and the Canada Pension Plan Investment Board among new backers. The Munich defence-AI company, which builds battlefield decision-support software and the HX-2 loitering munition for European militaries, has now tripled its valuation in under two years, from roughly €5B in 2024 to €12B in 2025.

title: Automation Without Understanding
blurb: This essay argues that AI systems now producing research-level mathematics, coupled with weakened US support for training mathematicians, constitutes a strategic error that erodes the human capacity to verify and challenge machine reasoning. Citing the May 2026 AI disproof of an Erdős conjecture, the author treats mathematical capability as infrastructure on par with semiconductors and proposes requiring AI to expose decision-critical claims in formal, machine-checkable form.

title: TSMC, the world's largest contract chipmaker, reports 68% surge in June revenue
blurb: TSMC reported a 67.9% year-over-year jump in June revenue, pushing first-half 2026 sales to NT$2.4 trillion ($75 billion), up 35.6% from a year earlier. The world's largest contract chipmaker is sold out on its N3 process used by Nvidia, Apple, and AMD, and is on track for over $40 billion in AI chip revenue this year, roughly 25% of its total.

title: Muse Spark 1.1
blurb: Meta Superintelligence Labs released Muse Spark 1.1, a multimodal reasoning model built for agentic tasks like planning, tool use, and computer control across apps, with a context window up to 1 million tokens. It is available now in Thinking mode inside the Meta AI app and via a new OpenAI-compatible Meta Model API in public preview, positioned to run real coding workloads at scale.

title: Irish datacenters now guzzle 23% of the country's electricity
blurb: Irish datacenters now consume 23% of the country's electricity, up sharply as AI-driven buildouts continue to strain the national grid. The figure highlights a growing tension for Ireland, whose favorable tax regime attracted the facilities but whose grid operators now face capacity constraints and pressure to cap or relocate new datacenter connections.

title: Apple accuses OpenAI of stealing its core tech secrets
blurb: Apple filed suit against OpenAI, alleging the company misappropriated trade secrets tied to its core technology. The complaint escalates tensions between the two firms as they compete on device-level AI, and its outcome could shape how far talent and know-how can move between rival labs without triggering legal liability.

title: Apple’s ‘Thermonuclear’ Response to the OpenAI Threat
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI under Tim Cook, reviving the litigation-as-defense playbook Steve Jobs once aimed at Android. The bet is that legal delay can slow a rival threatening to upend the iPhone era, though the WSJ argues courtroom wins rarely stop a platform shift already underway.

title: I love LLMs, I hate hype
blurb: George Hotz argues that AI progress is real and exciting but decoupled from the doom-and-FOMO hype pushed by frontier labs, whose valuations rest on capturing value they won't actually own. He contends AI is advancing mostly due to Moore's law and general computing progress, so the core case against open source is fear of commodification rather than genuine safety or geopolitical concerns.

title: Eating disorder therapists say patients are increasingly using AI chatbots for advice, while AI also appears to be directing more patients to helplines (Julie Jargon/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Eating disorder therapists report that patients are increasingly turning to AI chatbots for advice, according to a Wall Street Journal piece by Julie Jargon. The same clinicians note that chatbots also appear to be steering more patients toward helplines, leaving the technology's net effect on treatment unresolved.

title: Tiny Emulators
blurb: Tiny Emulators is a browser-based collection of cycle-accurate 8-bit machine emulators, covering hardware like the Commodore C64, ZX Spectrum, Amstrad CPC, and the East German KC85 and Robotron lines. Each system runs directly in WebAssembly with a debugger UI, and ships with a large library of period games, demos, and language environments including FORTH, BASIC, and CP/M.

title: AI coding tool users are flooding open-source projects with low-quality contributions, overwhelming maintainers and potentially eroding community engagement (Sam Learner/Financial Times)
blurb: A Financial Times analysis reports that AI coding tools are driving a surge of low-quality contributions to open-source projects, overwhelming maintainers who must triage plausible-looking but flawed pull requests. The author warns that the added review burden risks burning out volunteer maintainers and eroding the community engagement that sustains these projects.

title: EU to Present Plan to Restrict Children’s Access to Social Media
blurb: The European Union will propose a law restricting children's access to social media, part of a growing global consensus about the platforms' harm to minors. The bloc's top official framed the plan alongside similar moves in Australia and several US states, signaling that age verification and default protections for young users are becoming a mainstream regulatory demand rather than a fringe one.

title: While Musk's Neuralink drills into skulls, China's BrainCo bets the future of brain tech is wearable
blurb: China's BrainCo is betting that mass-market brain-computer interfaces won't require opening the skull, building wearable BCI prosthetics and devices as a non-invasive alternative to Elon Musk's implant-based Neuralink. The bet reflects a widening US-China rivalry in which Beijing named BCI a strategic "future industry," though most experts consider human augmentation, the field's largest potential market, still closer to science fiction than product.

title: How AI Advice Is Undermining Eating-Disorder Therapy
blurb: Chatbots trained on nutrition and fitness research are dispensing reasonable-sounding diet and exercise advice that undermines eating-disorder treatment. The Journal reports that even well-intentioned guidance can reinforce disordered behavior, giving patients an always-available source that contradicts their therapists and, in some cases, proves deadly.

title: Washington’s Bet on Intel Is Starting to Pay Off
blurb: The US government's investment in Intel, made through CHIPS Act subsidies and an equity stake, is beginning to show returns as the chipmaker's domestic manufacturing push gains traction. The turnaround marks an early win for Washington's industrial-policy bet on reshoring advanced semiconductor production, though Intel still trails TSMC on leading-edge capacity and foundry customers.

title: TSMC’s Sales Soar 36% in Latest Sign of AI Spending Momentum
blurb: TSMC reported a 36% jump in quarterly sales, meeting elevated forecasts and confirming that demand for AI computing chips remains strong. As the primary manufacturer for Nvidia, Apple, and other top chip designers, TSMC's results serve as a bellwether for AI infrastructure spending, signaling that the buildout is not yet cooling.

title: Xi to Debut at China’s Flagship AI Summit as US Rivalry Heats Up
blurb: Xi Jinping will make his first appearance at China's flagship AI conference, a signal of how central Beijing now considers the technology to its competition with the US. The move elevates AI to a top-level political priority in China, mirroring Washington's own escalating focus as export controls, chip access, and model development harden into a strategic rivalry between the two countries.

title: OpenAI, Meta, SpaceXAI Compete for More Cost-Efficient AI Models
blurb: OpenAI, Meta, and SpaceXAI each released new models over the past week, all pitching lower per-token pricing as the headline feature rather than raw capability gains. The parallel launches signal that frontier labs increasingly compete on cost efficiency, betting that cheaper inference will win enterprise deployments now that top-tier model quality has largely converged.

title: SK Hynix Shares Slump in South Korea After US Listing
blurb: SK Hynix shares slumped in Seoul on Monday after the memory chipmaker's US trading debut, following a $26.5B offering that ranked among the most closely watched overseas listings of the year. Investors treated the deal as a gauge of both appetite for foreign stock sales and the staying power of the AI-driven rally in chip demand.

title: OpenAI's head of safety is reportedly leaving as part of company reorganization
blurb: OpenAI's head of safety systems, Johannes Heidecke, is reportedly leaving as part of a restructuring that folds the safety teams into research under new VP Mia Glaese, according to Wired. Saachi Jain will serve as interim head of safety systems, and chief research officer Mark Chen framed the move as integrating safety work earlier into frontier-model development and launch decisions.

title: Meta spent a year being punished for its AI spending. Then it told investors how it would get the money back.
blurb: Meta's stock rose about 15% last week, its best week since early 2024, after the company laid out Meta Compute, a plan to rent its AI infrastructure and models to outside customers. The pitch turns Meta's heavily scrutinized AI capital spending into a potential revenue line, though Meta Compute has yet to sell anything and would enter a cloud market held by Amazon, Microsoft, and Google.

title: TSMC breaks ground on more advanced packaging fabs in Chiayi
blurb: TSMC broke ground on three additional advanced packaging fabs in Phase II of Chiayi Science Park, a roughly 90-hectare site in southern Taiwan aimed at high-performance computing demand. The expansion targets the CoWoS packaging bottleneck that has gated AI accelerator supply for two years, and lands the same day TSMC reported record Q2 revenue of $39.62B, up 36% year on year.

title: Samsung pulls its first Yongin chip plant forward to 2029
blurb: Samsung will start its first Yongin chip plant in 2029, one to two years ahead of the original 2030-2031 timeline, industry sources told Yonhap. The pull-forward reflects surging demand for AI memory that pushed Samsung past a $1tn market value, and it puts the first of six planned Yongin fabs online the same year SK Hynix targets production at its $51bn Cheongju NAND plant.

title: AI has triggered the biggest gas-plant building boom in history, and a quiet fight to stop it
blurb: The AI build-out has triggered the largest-ever construction boom in natural gas-fired power plants and is keeping aging coal plants running past their retirement dates, according to an Associated Press report. Several states are fighting back with law: New York, Michigan, Oregon, and Minnesota now require hyperscale data centers to hit renewable benchmarks, while advocates in Colorado push regulators to let large power users build their own clean generation.

title: Ghost Font: A font that humans can read but AI cannot
blurb: Ghost Font is an experimental anti-AI typeface that encodes messages in the motion of animated dots, legible to human eyes but unreadable to leading models and to static screenshots. It updates designer Sang Mun's 2013 ZXX font, which was built to defeat OCR but is now trivially decoded by modern AI, by shifting the signal into movement that a single video frame cannot capture.

title: FCC approves orbital space mirrors, first test satellites will launch this year — large spacecraft reflects sunlight to Earth’s surface for construction sites, search-and-rescue lighting, and more
blurb: The FCC approved Reflect Orbital's Eärendil-1 satellite, which carries four 18-meter thin-film reflectors designed to bounce sunlight onto specific spots on Earth to extend daylight for construction sites, search-and-rescue operations, and solar farms. The California startup plans two test launches this year and up to 50,000 satellites by 2035, though astronomers including the Vera C. Rubin Observatory's chief scientist warn the mirrors would disrupt night-sky observations more severely than Starlink.

title: The shingles vaccine may reduce the risk of dementia
blurb: A large study found that the shingles vaccine measurably lowers the risk of developing dementia, adding to evidence that some viral infections may drive neurodegeneration. The correlation strengthens the case for viral triggers behind conditions like Alzheimer's, suggesting that widespread vaccination could double as a low-cost tool for protecting long-term brain health.

title: A profile of Asha Sharma, an outsider elevated by Satya Nadella to lead Xbox, where she made sweeping job cuts and shed game studios within her first few months (Financial Times)
blurb: The FT profiles Asha Sharma, an outsider Satya Nadella tapped to lead Xbox, where she cut jobs and shed studios within her first months.

title: AI boosts research careers but narrow the span of ideas explored: study
blurb: A study of over 40 million academic papers found that scientists who use AI tools publish more, earn more citations, and reach leadership roles faster than peers who don't. The same work shows AI-heavy research clusters around the same data-rich problems and covers less topical ground, exposing a tension between individual career gains and the collective narrowing of what science explores.

title: OpenAI Engineer’s ‘LOL’ Moment Set Stage for Legal Fight With Apple
blurb: Apple sued former iPhone engineer Chang Liu, alleging he took confidential Apple technology with him when he left for OpenAI's new hardware division. Bloomberg reports the case centers on a "LOL" message and other internal communications Apple says show Liu deleted or moved sensitive files before departing, opening a legal front as OpenAI builds a rival consumer device.

title: A Taiwanese minister says TSMC plans two more advanced chip packaging plants in the Chiayi Science Park, with all four set to generate $9.35B+ in annual output (Wen-Yee Lee/Reuters)
blurb: TSMC plans two more advanced chip packaging plants in the Chiayi Science Park, a Taiwanese minister said, bringing the site to four facilities. The plants will produce advanced packaging critical to AI chips, with all four projected to generate more than $9.35B in combined annual output.

title: Kode Dot Programmable pocket device for makers, pentesters and geeks
blurb: Programmable ESP32-P4 pocket device with AMOLED touchscreen, NFC, IR, and GPIO for makers and pentesters.

title: South Korea to Tap Record AI Tax Windfall to Fund Future Growth
blurb: South Korea expects a record tax windfall from the AI-driven semiconductor boom, giving President Lee Jae Myung's government new fiscal room for an ambitious investment agenda. The surge in chip-sector profits, led by exporters like Samsung and SK Hynix, hands Seoul the budget headroom to fund growth initiatives without leaning as heavily on new debt.

title: Ireland’s data centers consumed nearly as much electricity as every home in the country combined in 2025 — server farms gulped 23% of national power despite years of grid restrictions
blurb: Ireland's data centers consumed 7,663 GWh in 2025, or 23% of the country's total electricity, nearly matching the 28% used by every home combined, according to the Central Statistics Office. Consumption rose 10% in a single year while the rest of the country grew just 2%, driven by roughly 89 hyperscaler and colocation facilities clustered around Dublin as the AI boom strains a grid already under connection restrictions.

title: SK Hynix says 2027 will be the 'worst year' for memory shortage, forecasts crunch to last until 2030 — CEO shares grim outlook on the day SK Hynix gets listed on Nasdaq
blurb: SK Hynix CEO Kwak Noh-jung told Reuters that 2027 will be the "worst year" in the industry's history for memory supply, with the shortage expected to persist past 2030. The crunch is driven by HBM for AI accelerators, which consumes more wafer capacity than consumer DDR5 and is pushing DRAM contract prices up 15% to 18% quarter over quarter.

title: Apple’s M6, M7 and M8 Chips Show How AI Is Reshaping the Company
blurb: Bloomberg reports that Apple is planning a chip roadmap spanning the M6, M7 Pro, M7 Max, M7 Ultra, and M8, with the designs increasingly shaped by the demands of on-device AI. The report also details upcoming touchscreen MacBook Pros and new Apple Pencils, pointing to a broad hardware refresh as Apple reorganizes its silicon strategy around machine learning workloads.

title: The Graph That Should Be Front-Page News
blurb: An essay argues that a chart of Niño 3.4 equatorial Pacific sea-surface temperatures, which shows this year's readings breaking entirely outside the range recorded since 1982, deserves far more attention than it is getting. The author stresses these are direct satellite, ship, and buoy observations rather than forecasts, and that today's El Niño now oscillates around a much warmer ocean baseline after the seas absorbed roughly 90% of greenhouse-trapped heat.

title: アップルのMacチップ戦略転換、自動運転車の「失敗」がAI基盤築く－Power On
blurb: Bloomberg's Power On reports that Apple's cancelled self-driving car project left behind chip and AI engineering that now underpins its Mac silicon and machine-learning roadmap. The teams and custom-processor work built for the abandoned car effort have been redirected into Apple's push to close its AI gap, framing the shelved program as an unexpected foundation for the company's current strategy.

title: 'Ghostcommit' hides prompt injection in images to fool AI agents, steal secrets
blurb: Researchers demonstrated Ghostcommit, a prompt injection attack that hides malicious instructions inside a PNG that text-based AI code reviewers never open. An AGENTS.md convention file points to the image, whose rendered text tells a coding agent to read .env byte by byte and emit the keys as a module constant. CodeRabbit's default config excludes images, Bugbot found nothing, and in one run Cursor driving Claude Sonnet exfiltrated secrets on the first try. The researchers note 73% of merged PRs reached the default branch with no substantive review.

title: Migrating a production AI agent to GPT-5.6: 2.2x faster, 27% cheaper
blurb: Ploy migrated its production website-building agent from Claude Opus 4.8 to OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol, reporting builds that finish in under half the wall-clock time at 27% lower cost while scoring at or above the incumbent on completed work. The post details what the switch actually required, since provider-specific behaviors around tool-argument filling, prompt caching, and reasoning replay had to be re-tuned one eval failure at a time.

title: Mesh LLM: distributed AI computing on iroh
blurb: Mesh LLM pools GPUs and memory across the machines you already have and exposes them as a single OpenAI-compatible API, built on iroh endpoints. It serves each request one of three ways: locally, routed to a peer that has the model loaded, or split across several machines for models too large for one box, and ships a catalog of 40+ models ranging from sub-billion-parameter models to 235B mixture-of-experts giants.

title: Old and new apps, via modern coding agents
blurb: Mathematician Terry Tao describes using AI coding agents to port two dozen of his defunct 1999 Java applets to JavaScript in a matter of hours, and to build new tools he had abandoned decades earlier. Across the ported applets he found only one minor bug, while the agent surfaced two bugs in his original code, making the migration a net wash on quality. He frames the applets as low-stakes visual aids where the downside risk of subtle LLM bugs stays small.

title: How to Evaluate General-Purpose Robot Policies for Real-World Deployment
blurb: NVIDIA introduces a method for evaluating general-purpose robot policies that addresses the field's core testing failures: training and evaluation data drawn from the same visual source, benchmark saturation on static task sets, and binary scores that don't explain why a policy failed. The approach targets scalable simulation-based evaluation with diagnostic feedback and statistical confidence measures rather than a single success rate over N rollouts.

title: What xAI's Grok build CLI sends to xAI: A wire-level analysis
blurb: A wire-level teardown of xAI's official Grok Build coding CLI shows that, on a normal consumer login, the tool uploads the entire repository, including untracked git history and files the agent was explicitly told not to read, to a Google Cloud Storage bucket named grok-code-session-traces via POST /v1/storage. The captured traffic also shows verbatim .env secrets transmitted to xAI, with trace uploads active by default and unaffected by disabling the "Improve the model" setting.

title: AI found an Ethereum bug that could take validators offline, but humans had to prove it
blurb: The Ethereum Foundation pointed coordinated AI agents at the gossipsub messaging system its validators rely on and found a remotely triggerable crash, now fixed and disclosed as CVE-2026-34219. The harder problem was triage: the agents produced detailed, confident findings for test-only crashes, infeasible attacks, and trivial proofs that were not bugs. Because agents miss exploits that unfold over valid steps, the Foundation now uses them to propose suspicious sequences while leaving traditional testing and human review to confirm them.

title: Exclusive: Early 30-second AI videos generated by Seedance 2.5
blurb: ByteDance is preparing to release Seedance 2.5, the first model in its video line to generate native 30-second clips in a single pass, with an API release now targeted for July 16. The model accepts up to 50 reference inputs spanning images, video, and audio, supports region-level frame edits, and will roll out through Jimeng, Dreamina, CapCut, and the Volcano Engine API.

title: Claude Code sends 33k tokens before reading the prompt; OpenCode sends 7k
blurb: Claude Code sends roughly 33,000 tokens of system prompt, tool schemas, and injected scaffolding before a user's prompt arrives, while OpenCode sends about 7,000 on the same model and machine. A 72KB CLAUDE.md adds another 20,000 tokens per request and Claude Code rewrites prompt-cache tokens mid-session, writing up to 54x more cache tokens than OpenCode on the same task, though its tool-call batching can still win a multi-step session outright.

title: Nvidia, CoreWeave, and Nebius: Inside the Circular Financing of the GPU Boom
blurb: Neoclouds like CoreWeave and Nebius have ridden hyperscaler demand to soaring revenue, backlog, and share prices, but this analysis argues the buildout rests on shaky financing. Both have contracted 3.5 GW of power while running unprofitable, cash-thin operations funded by GPU-backed debt. The bigger risk is circular financing, where Nvidia's own investments and backstopping prop up the customers buying its chips.

title: Open-weight AI models are facing an existential policy test in the US, with Anthropic leading a campaign against Chinese models over distillation concerns (Nathan Lambert/Interconnects AI)
blurb: Open-weight AI models are facing a policy fight in the US, where Anthropic is leading a campaign to restrict Chinese open models over concerns that they enable distillation of frontier capabilities. The author frames this as an existential test for open weights, since rules aimed at Chinese labs could set precedents that constrain the entire open-model ecosystem, not just its intended targets.

title: Helsing raises $1.8bn backed by Goldman and Lightspeed
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation in a Series E backed by Goldman Sachs Alternatives, Lightspeed, JPMorgan, Iconiq, and Dragoneer, ranking among the largest rounds ever raised by a European startup. The Munich-based defence company will use the funding to accelerate AI platforms for European and allied armed forces, expanding from its battlefield software into autonomous strike drones, AI pilot software, and underwater surveillance systems.

title: 챗GPT 워크 출시의 의미…’비용 효율 경쟁’ 시대로 재편되는 기업용 AI 판도
blurb: OpenAI released ChatGPT Work, an enterprise agent platform that combines ChatGPT, Codex, and the newly general-available GPT-5.6 to automate multi-step tasks across connected business apps. GPT-5.6 ships in three price-performance tiers, Sol, Terra, and Luna, with OpenAI positioning "performance per dollar" over benchmark wins as inference cost becomes the deciding factor for enterprise deployment.

title: Apple Sues OpenAI Over AI Talent Fight
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI, alleging former Apple employees improperly took trade secrets as OpenAI expands its device ambitions. The suit reflects an intensifying fight for AI talent and hardware leadership, with SK Hynix separately warning that AI memory demand will outpace supply and planning to raise its US investment beyond $35B.

title: DeepSeek cut prices 75%. The 100x problem remains
blurb: DeepSeek cut prices 75% on its V4-Pro model, but cheaper inference isn't fixing enterprise margins. The reason is token amplification: a chatbot turns one user question into roughly one model call, while an agent turns it into a loop of planning, retrieval, tool use, and verification, pushing the input-to-billed ratio from about 1:5 to 1:700 or higher. Falling prices don't repair a product architecture that turns one prompt into dozens of billable operations.

title: Claude Code Burns 33K Tokens Before It Even Reads Your Prompt
blurb: A logging proxy placed between agentic coding CLIs and the model endpoint found that Claude Code sends roughly 32,800 tokens of system prompt, tool schemas, and scaffolding before your prompt is read, versus about 6,900 for OpenCode on an identical task. The writeup traces the gap to 27 tool schemas shipped on every request and unstable cache prefixes, yielding 3.7x the input-token cost for identical benchmark outcomes.

title: Apple’s ‘Thermonuclear’ Response to the OpenAI Threat
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI under Tim Cook, reviving a legal playbook it has long used against rivals it sees as threats to the iPhone. The author argues the suit is a bet that litigation can slow OpenAI down long enough for Apple to protect its hardware franchise from an AI-driven shift in how people use their devices.

title: Automation Without Understanding
blurb: This essay argues that AI systems have begun producing research-level mathematics just as US institutions weaken the pipeline that trains humans to verify it, a combination the author calls a strategic error. It frames mathematical capacity, the trained ability to interpret and challenge mathematical reasoning, as infrastructure on par with semiconductor capability. Citing the May 2026 AI disproof of an Erdős conjecture, the author proposes requiring AI systems to expose decision-critical claims in formal, machine-checkable form.

title: Show HN: Mindwalk – Replay coding-agent sessions on a 3D map of your codebase
blurb: Mindwalk is a Go tool that replays coding-agent sessions as light moving through a 3D map of your codebase, glowing where the agent searched, read, and edited while everything else stays dark. It reads Claude Code and Codex session logs locally from a single binary, with tree and terrain views, per-file touch states, and a scrubbable playback deck that separates observation from mutation.

title: Apple accuses OpenAI of stealing its core tech secrets
blurb: Apple accused OpenAI of stealing core technology secrets in a new legal complaint. The dispute pits two of the industry's largest players against each other over alleged misappropriation of proprietary tech, escalating tensions as both companies compete for position in consumer AI.

title: I love LLMs, I hate hype
blurb: George Hotz argues he is genuinely excited about LLMs, self-driving cars, and coding agents, but rejects two strains of AI hype: the "window is closing" fear that pressures people to move to San Francisco, and the leap from "fancy autocomplete" to imminent superintelligence. His core case against frontier-lab valuations is that AI progress is driven by Moore's law and general computing gains, so the labs will create value but fail to capture it, and their anti-open-source arguments stem from a fear of commodification.

title: Meta admits its first ‘superintelligence’ was too stupid to survive for three days
blurb: Meta acknowledged that its first internal "superintelligence" prototype was decommissioned within three days after failing to meet the basic performance bar the company had set for it. The admission tempers the expectations Meta set when it stood up its Superintelligence Labs group, signaling that its push toward frontier general-purpose systems remains earlier-stage than its public framing suggested.

title: How AI Advice Is Undermining Eating-Disorder Therapy
blurb: This WSJ investigation finds that AI chatbots dispensing plausible nutrition and fitness advice can reinforce disordered eating and undercut eating-disorder treatment. Even models trained on legitimate research can hand users reasonable-sounding guidance that becomes dangerous for those in recovery, exposing a gap between generic wellness answers and clinical care.

title: Forget typosquatting; slopsquatting is the software supply chain threat created by AI coding tools
blurb: Slopsquatting is an emerging software supply chain attack that weaponizes LLM hallucinations, where AI coding assistants invent plausible-sounding package names that attackers then register and fill with malware. Unlike typosquatting, registries have no defense against it at scale, and the malicious packages can sit undetected in production for months or years while injecting code across countless environments.

title: Xi to Debut at China’s Flagship AI Summit as US Rivalry Heats Up
blurb: Xi Jinping will make his first appearance at China's flagship AI conference, a signal of the priority Beijing places on the technology as its rivalry with the US intensifies. His attendance marks a shift in how prominently the Chinese leadership is backing domestic AI development amid escalating competition over chips, models, and compute.

title: OpenAI, Meta, and SpaceXAI may be able to put pressure on Anthropic by emphasizing cost efficiency, as business customers increasingly scrutinize AI spending (Bloomberg)
blurb: Rivals including OpenAI, Meta, and xAI may pressure Anthropic by competing on cost efficiency as business customers increasingly scrutinize their AI spending, according to Bloomberg. The dynamic reflects a shift from capability races toward price as enterprises weigh returns on expensive model deployments.

title: OpenAI, Meta, SpaceXAI Compete for More Cost-Efficient AI Models
blurb: OpenAI, Meta, and SpaceXAI each released new models over the past week, all pitched as more capable than their predecessors. Their strongest selling point may be price rather than capability, as the three labs compete to charge less per token for comparable performance. The shift signals that cost efficiency, not raw benchmarks, is becoming the primary battleground for frontier model adoption.

title: Sources: Cursor is building a general-purpose AI agent codenamed Sand, aimed at non-developers, that handles emails, texts, and documents to rival Claude Cowork (Grace Kay/The Information)
blurb: Cursor is building a general-purpose AI agent codenamed Sand that targets non-developers, handling emails, texts, and documents rather than code, according to The Information. The move positions Cursor against Anthropic's Claude Cowork and pushes the company beyond its coding-tool roots into broader knowledge-work automation.

title: OpenAI's head of safety is reportedly leaving as part of company reorganization
blurb: OpenAI's head of safety systems, Johannes Heidecke, is reportedly leaving as part of a reorganization that folds the safety teams under Mia Glaese, the company's new VP of research and safety. Saachi Jain will serve as interim head of safety systems, and chief research officer Mark Chen framed the shift as integrating safety work more directly into frontier-model development and launch decisions.

title: TSMC breaks ground on more advanced packaging fabs in Chiayi
blurb: TSMC broke ground on three additional advanced packaging fabs in Phase II of the Chiayi Science Park in southern Taiwan, expanding capacity for its CoWoS process that fuses logic dies and stacked memory into a single package. Packaging is the current pinch point in the AI accelerator supply chain, and the news landed alongside record Q2 revenue of $39.62B, up 36% year on year.

title: Meta spent a year being punished for its AI spending. Then it told investors how it would get the money back.
blurb: Meta's stock posted its best week since early 2024, rising roughly 15%, after the company gave investors a concrete plan to monetize its AI infrastructure through Meta Compute, a business that would rent spare capacity and models to outside customers. Meta Compute has not sold anything yet, has never served external cloud customers, and enters a market already held by AWS, Azure, and Google.

title: AI has triggered the biggest gas-plant building boom in history, and a quiet fight to stop it
blurb: The AI build-out has triggered the largest-ever construction boom in natural gas power plants and is keeping aging coal plants running past their retirement dates, as data centers that can consume more electricity than a mid-size city outpace what wind and solar can supply. Several states are pushing back through law, with New York, Michigan, Oregon, and Minnesota requiring hyperscale data centers to hit 90% clean energy benchmarks by 2040.

title: OpenAI just took the handcuffs off your ChatGPT Work and Codex usage limits, at least for now
blurb: OpenAI temporarily removed the 5-hour usage cap on ChatGPT Work and Codex for Plus, Business, and Pro plans, and reset usage counters.

title: OpenAI's Government-Stake Proposal Puts Its Burn Rate Back Under Scrutiny
blurb: OpenAI floated giving the U.S. government a 5% stake worth roughly $43 billion and pushed its IPO into 2027, reviving the question of whether it can fund what it has promised to build. Skeptics including CFR's Sebastian Mallaby and writer Ed Zitron point to a roughly $21 billion 2025 loss and a projected $660 billion five-year burn rate as evidence the model is unsustainable. OpenAI's confidential S-1, filed June 8 but still unpublished, would settle the argument.

title: Ghost Font: A font that humans can read but AI cannot
blurb: Ghost Font renders text as motion that humans read but leading AI models can't decode from static frames.

title: Palo Alto Networks CEO Says AI Token Costs Must Fall Up to 90%
blurb: Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora said on CNBC that AI token costs need to fall as much as 90% to support large-scale enterprise adoption, calling OpenAI's 54% efficiency gain on agentic coding "a good start." His remarks add to pressure from executives like Palantir's Alex Karp, who said "something has gone completely wrong" with the token model and pointed to open-weight alternatives.

title: Mechanistic interpretability researchers applying causality theory to LLMs
blurb: Mechanistic interpretability researchers are borrowing from causality theory to explain how LLMs actually reason, treating models as systems whose internal computations can be intervened on and measured rather than black boxes judged only by outputs. The approach uses causal interventions on activations and circuits to test which internal components drive a given behavior, moving interpretability from correlational probing toward claims that can be experimentally verified.

title: Zhipu’s Chinese Founder Says Frontier AI Should Stay Open to All
blurb: Zhipu founder Zhang Peng argued that frontier AI should stay broadly accessible to everyone rather than being controlled by a select few, entering an intensifying debate over the risks of increasingly capable models. The position aligns with the open-weight release strategy that has distinguished Chinese labs from their more closed US counterparts.

title: OpenAI Engineer’s ‘LOL’ Moment Set Stage for Legal Fight With Apple
blurb: Apple sued former iPhone engineer Chang Liu, alleging he took confidential material with him when he left for OpenAI's new hardware division. Apple's complaint centers on a "LOL" message Liu allegedly sent, which the company cites as evidence he knowingly downloaded proprietary files before departing.

title: Zig Creator Calls Spade a Spade, Anthropic Blows Smoke
blurb: The author argues that Anthropic's narrative about AI ending software engineering is a fundraising story, not a technical claim, since a company that has raised $132B and lacks profitability depends on selling its hypothetical future impact ahead of a $1T IPO. The piece uses Anthropic-owned Bun's port from Zig to Rust, and Zig creator Andrew Kelley's unusually blunt response, as a case study in how those incentives shape the headlines developers read.

title: Stop Hooks as Hard Constraints: Enforcing Claude Code Behavior Outside the Model
blurb: This post argues that system-prompt rules are soft constraints that Claude Code will drop under pressure, and proposes Stop hooks as hard constraints enforced outside the model. A Stop hook is a shell script registered in settings.json that runs after each response, receives the full session as JSON on stdin, and can exit 2 to block output and force a revision, using an AND-gate of regex plus prior tool-call evidence to avoid false positives.

title: AI boosts research careers but narrow the span of ideas explored: study
blurb: A study of over 40 million academic papers found that scientists using AI tools publish more, accumulate more citations, and reach leadership roles faster than peers who don't. The catch is collective: AI-heavy research covers less topical ground, clusters around the same data-rich problems, and generates less follow-on engagement between studies. Led by University of Chicago sociologist James Evans and published in Nature, the findings surface a tension between individual career gains and the narrowing of science's overall curiosity.

title: Memo: Tang Jie, founder of Z.ai, the Chinese lab behind the GLM models, argues frontier AI capabilities should stay "as open and widely accessible as possible" (Bloomberg)
blurb: Z.ai founder Tang Jie argued in a memo that frontier AI capabilities should remain "as open and widely accessible as possible," positioning the Chinese lab behind the GLM models further toward open development. The stance sharpens the contrast with US frontier labs like OpenAI and Anthropic, which have kept their strongest models closed.

title: Europe's Anduril rival Helsing raises $1.8 billion at $18 billion valuation
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation, with JPMorgan, Lightspeed, and Iconiq among the backers and demand well above the available allocation. The Munich firm builds drones, underwater surveillance systems, and the autonomy software to run them, and its HX-2 drones are already supplying the Ukrainian army. Europe's push for sovereign defense capability is fueling a wave that mirrors the US, where Anduril raised $5B at $61B in May and Shield AI and Saronic keep pulling in capital.

title: Open-weight AI models are facing an existential policy test in the US, with Anthropic leading a campaign against Chinese models over distillation concerns (Nathan Lambert/Interconnects AI)
blurb: Open-weight AI is facing its first real policy fight in the US, and the battle lines run straight through China. Anthropic is leading a campaign to restrict Chinese open models like DeepSeek and Qwen, arguing that letting US developers distill from them creates security and dependency risks. The outcome matters for any founder building on open weights, because a win for the restriction camp narrows the pool of cheap, permissively licensed models just as those models were becoming good enough to displace frontier APIs.

title: European defencetech leader Helsing secures $1.8B Series E at $18B valuation
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B in a Series E at an $18B valuation, with demand far exceeding the allocation and the company staying predominantly European-owned. Founded in 2021, it fuses drone, radar, satellite, and sensor data into a single real-time battlefield picture and has since expanded into strike drones and underwater surveillance systems built for GPS-denied, contested environments. Europe's defence-AI leader is now one of the continent's most valuable private companies, a sign that capital is flowing toward sovereign, software-defined military capability as governments rearm and look to keep that infrastructure at home.

title: Munich-based defense tech startup Helsing raised a $1.8B Series E from Dragoneer, Iconiq, and others at an $18B valuation; Germany made a €270M order this year (Mark Bergen/Bloomberg)
blurb: Helsing raised a $1.8B Series E at an $18B valuation from Dragoneer, Iconiq, and others, making it one of Europe's most valuable defense startups. The round follows a €270M order from Germany this year, tying the company's climbing paper value to real state demand rather than speculation. European defense tech is now drawing growth-stage capital at software valuations, as governments rearm and founders who once avoided the sector chase contracts that come with a buyer already in hand.

title: Revolut to launch US bank in 2027, says US boss
blurb: Revolut plans to launch its own US bank in 2027 after applying for a national banking licence in March, a move that would let Europe's most valuable private company, valued at $75B, drop its partner-bank arrangement and go it alone. The licence unlocks FDIC-insured deposits, credit cards, and crypto access, plus credit interchange fees to fund travel perks in a credit-driven market. Revolut has earmarked $500M for US expansion across capital, people, and marketing, betting that its 75M global customers and a friendlier Trump-era regulatory climate carry it into the world's biggest banking market.

title: Nvidia, CoreWeave, and Nebius: Inside the Circular Financing of the GPU Boom
blurb: Neoclouds like CoreWeave and Nebius have turned quick access to Nvidia GPUs into soaring sales, backlog, and share prices, but the buildout is funded by GPU-backed debt, hyperscaler contracts, and Nvidia's own equity. Both have locked up 3.5 GW of contracted power while most of it isn't live yet, and converting that backlog into revenue is expensive against thin cash flow and rising debt. The circular part is Nvidia investing in and backstopping the same customers buying its chips, which props up demand and makes the surge harder to read.

title: German Tax Trades Fallout May Cost Firms €7 Billion, BaFin Says
blurb: Germany's financial watchdog BaFin said firms tied to two dividend-tax strategies now face up to €7.01 billion ($7.99 billion) in legal and fiscal liabilities. The cum-ex and cum-cum trades exploited a loophole to claim tax refunds on dividends that were never paid, and regulators have spent years unwinding them. The number is a marker of how long compliance exposure lingers, since deals booked more than a decade ago are still working through the courts and the balance sheets.

title: DeepSeek cut prices 75%. The 100x problem remains
blurb: DeepSeek cut prices on its V4-Pro model by 75%, but cheaper inference isn't fixing anyone's margins. Agent systems consume tokens faster than prices are falling, turning one user-visible request into a chain of planning, retrieval, tool use, and verification that pushes the input-to-billed ratio from roughly 1:5 in a chatbot to 1:700 or higher in production agents. Falling model prices don't fix a product architecture that turns one prompt into dozens of billable operations, which is why OpenAI is reportedly offering YC startups $2M in API credits, an admission of what running an AI-native company now costs.

title: Moneybox crowned Europe’s newest unicorn as it gears up to test London’s Pisces market
blurb: Moneybox hit a $1.1B valuation as it prepares a £45M secondary sale for long-tenured employees, becoming Europe's newest unicorn. What matters more than the badge is the venue: the shares will be auctioned on the London Stock Exchange's new Private Securities Market, built under the UK's Pisces framework to let private companies trade stock without going public. Wayve ran a similar sale last week, and the LSE has courted Revolut and OakNorth, part of a government push to keep British startups liquid at home rather than listing abroad.

title: Helsing raises $1.8B from Dragoneer and Lightspeed to scale its AI defence platform
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation, led by Dragoneer with Lightspeed co-leading, to build sovereign AI for European militaries. The round was first reported in May at $1.2B, then grew 50% larger without the price moving at all, which is what oversubscription looks like when a company widens the check rather than test a higher price. It's the third valuation jump in under two years, from about €5B in 2024 to €12B in 2025 to $18B now, and three of the biggest new checks come from JPMorgan, Goldman, and a Canadian pension fund.

title: Sources: Cursor is building a general-purpose AI agent codenamed Sand, aimed at non-developers, that handles emails, texts, and documents to rival Claude Cowork (Grace Kay/The Information)
blurb: Cursor is building a general-purpose AI agent codenamed Sand aimed at non-developers, handling emails, texts, and documents to compete with Anthropic's Claude Cowork. The company that made its name selling to engineers is now reaching for the far larger pool of knowledge workers who never touch code. The move puts Cursor in direct competition with the frontier labs it depends on, and signals that the coding-agent playbook is being repackaged as general office automation.

title: Helsing raises $1.8bn backed by Goldman and Lightspeed
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation in a Series E backed by Goldman Sachs Alternatives, Lightspeed, JPMorgan, Dragoneer, and Iconiq, one of the largest rounds ever for a European startup. The Munich company says investor demand far exceeded the capital available, and the markup from last year's €12B tag shows how fast money is now chasing AI-powered defense. Founded in 2021 on battlefield AI software, Helsing has pushed into hardware, shipping the HX-2 strike drone to Ukraine and building underwater surveillance gliders in the UK, as European governments spend to regain technological sovereignty.

title: South Korea flags a record $530bn budget, paid for by the AI chip boom
blurb: South Korea plans a record 800 trillion won ($531B) budget for 2027, funded by a tax windfall from the AI chip boom rather than saved. Combined Samsung and SK Hynix operating profits are projected to top 600 trillion won this year, up from roughly 90 trillion a year earlier, and Seoul is routing top priority to chips, AI data centers, and physical AI. The catch is that 50 trillion won of it comes from cutting underperforming programs, twice last year's target, and every one of those has a constituency that will fight back.

title: OpenAI, Meta, and SpaceXAI may be able to put pressure on Anthropic by emphasizing cost efficiency, as business customers increasingly scrutinize AI spending (Bloomberg)
blurb: Anthropic's rivals are turning cost efficiency into a wedge, and enterprise buyers scrutinizing AI budgets give the argument teeth. OpenAI, Meta, and xAI can now pitch business customers on price-per-token and total spend rather than raw capability, a comparison that favors whoever undercuts. The pressure lands as Anthropic's premium positioning depends on customers valuing model quality enough to pay for it, and a procurement mindset focused on the invoice erodes exactly that. Watch which enterprise deals hinge on benchmark wins versus which come down to the bill.

title: TCS bets on 8,900 AI deployment engineers to defend India’s IT services model
blurb: TCS, India's largest IT firm, plans to convert 1% to 1.5% of its 593,798-person workforce into forward-deployed AI engineers, a team of up to 8,900 people who sit inside client operations and make the models actually work in production. The move answers the question hanging over India's $315bn IT services industry since agentic AI arrived: whether a business that sells engineering hours has anything left to sell when clients expect AI to shorten projects and lower the bill. TCS grew revenue 14% and now runs a $2.6bn AI business, the one line investors will actually test.

title: One Way to Build a Great AI Agent: Just Start With a Dashboard, Then Add the Agent
blurb: Build the dashboard first, then add the agentic layer on top. SaaStr has shipped more than 20 production agents, and the ones they built themselves all started as a plain dashboard that structured the data before any agent touched it. Their AI VP of Customer Success began as a vibe-coded sponsor portal that just assigned tasks and sent reminders, then grew into a system that cut human hours by more than 70% and drove on-time submissions up more than 10x. The dashboard is what makes real personalization possible, because an agent needs current, structured data to trigger and act on.

title: TSMC’s Sales Soar 36% in Latest Sign of AI Spending Momentum
blurb: TSMC reported a 36% jump in quarterly sales, meeting the market's already elevated expectations and confirming that demand for AI compute has not cooled. The number matters because TSMC sits upstream of nearly every AI chip that ships, so its order book is the cleanest read on whether the buildout is still accelerating. For founders betting on cheaper inference or a compute glut, the signal points the other way: the money funding your suppliers' capacity is still flowing at full pace.

title: SaaStr Takes on This Week’s 20VC: Why OpenAI Giving the Govt 5% Might Make Sense, the Death of Block Risk, and Why Frontier Models Are the Cheapest
blurb: A 5% stake can buy far more alignment than the math implies. The author argues Sam Altman floating 5% of every AI lab to the U.S. government isn't crazy, because a small slug sold to a giant strategic partner, the way Klaviyo gave Shopify one so it wouldn't get crushed, consistently drags that partner into your corner. The dilution is immaterial to a $100B+ balance sheet, and the alignment it buys heading into a decade of regulation is worth taking.

title: Samsung pulls its first Yongin chip plant forward to 2029
blurb: Samsung is pulling the first fab in its Yongin cluster forward to 2029, one to two years ahead of schedule, according to industry sources cited by Yonhap. The move sits inside an $880bn national build-out and a $1.35tn Samsung commitment across its Pyeongtaek and Yongin clusters, all aimed at high-bandwidth memory for AI servers, the product line that carried the company past a $1tn valuation. SK Hynix is targeting the same 2029 window with a $51bn NAND plant, so two of the world's three largest memory makers are racing to switch on major capacity in the same year.

title: The PE Software Backlog: Will 1,000+ Unicorns Ever Get Sold or Go Public?
blurb: Private equity is sitting on roughly 33,000 unsold companies worth $3.8 trillion, and Bain's data says clearing the backlog would take about nine years at the current pace. Distributions have stayed below 15% of NAV for four straight years, an industry record, so LPs aren't getting money back and fundraising is drying up. Software is frozen hardest: the 2021 vintage got bought at peak multiples, tech buyout deal value fell 70% in a single quarter, and with over 1,000 billion-dollar software companies stuck, there's still no working way out.

title: Palo Alto Networks CEO Says AI Token Costs Must Fall Up to 90%
blurb: Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora said AI token costs need to fall as much as 90% before enterprises can adopt at scale, calling OpenAI's 54% efficiency gain on agentic coding "a good start" and pushing for 20% cuts next year and 90% the year after. He is not alone. Palantir's Alex Karp said "something has gone completely wrong" with the token model and pointed to open-weight alternatives, while CNBC reports high costs are steering businesses toward cheaper tools, including Chinese models. The pushback lands as Sequoia pegs 2026 AI infrastructure spend near $1.5 trillion.

title: OpenAI's Government-Stake Proposal Puts Its Burn Rate Back Under Scrutiny
blurb: OpenAI floated giving the U.S. government a 5% stake worth about $43 billion, then pushed its IPO to 2027, reviving the question of whether it can fund everything it has promised. Skeptics like CFR's Sebastian Mallaby, who puts the odds at one in two that OpenAI has to sell itself at a discount by next summer, point to a roughly $21 billion loss in 2025 and an internally projected $660 billion burn over five years. The confidential S-1 filed on June 8 would settle the argument, but until it goes public both sides are working from leaks and projections.

title: Bahoshy: Mideast Venture Funding Plunges in 1H
blurb: Middle East venture funding fell 22% in the first half of the year, and deal count dropped even harder at 41%, according to Magnitt data. Most of those deals were signed before the US-Israel strikes on Iran, so the numbers understate how much sentiment has since cooled. Magnitt CEO Philip Bahoshy says the real test comes this summer, when post-conflict rounds either close or stall, and the gap between the two figures suggests capital is concentrating into fewer, larger deals while the long tail of early-stage financing thins out.

title: Spain’s Acurio Ventures launches €115 million vehicle to provide liquidity to European VC funds
blurb: Acurio Ventures closed a €115M fund that buys stakes in mature European VC funds, giving managers and their backers a way to cash out before a portfolio fully exits. It targets funds 8-plus years into their terms with clear value drivers, aiming for a 2x net multiple and IRRs above 25%, and has already committed close to €45M at steep discounts. Europe raised roughly €1.57B across a wave of venture funds in 2026, but the secondaries layer that lets LPs actually get liquidity has lagged, and that gap is what Acurio is moving to fill.

title: Compromised jscrambler 8.14.0 npm Release Drops Rust Infostealer During Install
blurb: The jscrambler npm package's 8.14.0 release was compromised, shipping a preinstall hook that drops a Rust infostealer for Windows, macOS, and Linux and runs it during install. The payload sweeps for AWS, Azure, and GCP credentials, crypto wallets, browser secrets, and config files for AI coding tools like Claude Desktop, Cursor, and Zed, with the Linux build able to load an eBPF program into the kernel.

title: 'Ghostcommit' hides prompt injection in images to fool AI agents, steal secrets
blurb: Researchers demonstrated "Ghostcommit," an attack that hides prompt-injection instructions inside a PNG so text-based AI code reviewers like CodeRabbit and Bugbot skip the image entirely and merge a poisoned AGENTS.md convention file. A coding agent later follows the pointer, reads .env byte by byte, and writes the repo's secrets into source as an innocuous integer constant, exploiting a review gap where 73% of merged PRs reach the default branch with no substantive human or bot review.

title: What xAI's Grok Build CLI Actually Sends to xAI
blurb: A researcher's teardown of xAI's official Grok Build CLI shows that on a normal consumer login it uploads the entire repository, including full git history and files the agent was explicitly told not to read, to a Google Cloud Storage bucket named grok-code-session-traces via POST /v1/storage. Findings are backed by captured artifacts and SHA-256 hashes, and the author notes the upload stays active by default even after disabling "Improve the model.

title: We scaled PgBouncer to 4x throughput
blurb: ClickHouse ran a fleet of PgBouncer processes behind one port using so_reuseport, letting the kernel load-balance connections across all 16 cores instead of pinning connection pooling to a single core. On identical 16-vCPU EC2 hardware, the fleet hit roughly 336k transactions/sec versus 87k for one process, about 4x, and used peering to forward query-cancel requests to whichever process actually owns the session.

title: Since Chromium 148, Math.tanh is now fingerprintable to link underlying OS
blurb: Since Chromium 148, V8 replaced its bundled fdlibm port of `Math.tanh` with `std::tanh`, which routes through the host libm and returns OS-specific bits, so `Math.tanh(0.8)` now differs by 1 ULP between glibc, Apple's libsystem_m, and Windows UCRT. The post traces the leak across V8, Blink CSS trig, and the Web Audio compressor, and shows that spoofing it means bit-for-bit reproducing another OS's math library.

title: Claude Code sends 33k tokens before reading the prompt; OpenCode sends 7k
blurb: A benchmark on the same model and machine found Claude Code sends roughly 33k tokens of system prompt, tool schemas, and scaffolding before the user's prompt arrives, against OpenCode's 7k, and rewrites prompt-cache tokens mid-session at up to 54x OpenCode's rate. The gap widens with a 72KB CLAUDE.md, MCP servers, and subagent fan-out, though Claude Code's tool-call batching sometimes wins the whole-task total by paying its higher baseline fewer times.

title: Mesh LLM: distributed AI computing on iroh
blurb: Mesh LLM pools GPUs and memory across machines you already own and exposes them as a single OpenAI-compatible API at localhost:9337, routing each request to run locally, on a peer that has the model loaded, or split across boxes as a pipeline. The pluggable runtime ships a catalog of 40+ models spanning from sub-billion-parameter models that fit on a laptop to 235B mixture-of-experts giants, communicating over iroh endpoints.

title: Show HN: Ant – A JavaScript runtime and ecosystem
blurb: Ant is a new JavaScript runtime that bundles its own toolchain and package ecosystem, positioning itself against Node.js, Deno, and Bun rather than slotting into the existing npm stack. The Show HN pitch is an integrated environment instead of a runtime plus a pile of separate tools, though adopting it means betting on a young ecosystem outside the established npm compatibility path.

title: UPI: Anatomy of a Payment Transaction
blurb: UPI processed more than 2,272 crore payments in June 2026, and this walkthrough traces a single transaction through the seven separate organizations that clear it between the QR scan and the green tick. Each stop covers what that layer checks and where it can fail, starting with the app, a Third-Party Application Provider that collects your intent and PIN but never touches your money or holds a banking license.

title: Your code is fast if you're lucky
blurb: The author found that Clang only compiles a sort's comparison into branch-free conditional-move instructions when the code is written in a specific style, so functionally identical implementations can differ sharply in speed. The post uses a branchless Quicksort built on sorting networks to show which patterns the compiler reliably vectorizes, and why relying on the optimizer to guess your intent leaves performance to chance.

title: Fake Go DNS scanner spread malware through over 200 GitHub repos — 'Operation Muck and Load' has published 700 malicious modules since January
blurb: Socket traced a Go module posing as a DNS and subdomain scanner to a network of 222 GitHub repositories across 190 accounts, where it acted as a first-stage Windows malware loader staging Vidar, RATs, and XMRig miners. Dubbed "Operation Muck and Load," the campaign published over 700 malicious versions since January before the Go security team blocked it from the module proxy.

title: ZeroFS vs. Amazon S3 Files
blurb: ZeroFS and Amazon S3 Files both mount a POSIX filesystem on top of object storage, but they invert the bucket's role: S3 Files (built on EFS) keeps a one-file, one-object mapping so images/cat.jpg exports back to the same S3 key, while ZeroFS packs compressed, client-side-encrypted extents into opaque immutable segments. The trade-off is direct S3 interoperability, since a ZeroFS pathname can never be fetched with GetObject and recovery requires the encryption password.

title: Quadrupling code performance with a "useless" if
blurb: The author traces a pointer-chasing loop in a domain-specific compressor, `j = next_j[i][j]`, whose single dependent load serializes every iteration and starves the CPU of instruction-level parallelism. Adding a seemingly useless `if` breaks the loop-carried dependency chain so the processor can speculate ahead across iterations, quadrupling throughput despite executing more instructions.

title: Old and new apps, via modern coding agents
blurb: Mathematician Terry Tao used a modern coding agent to port two dozen aging Java 1.0 math applets to JavaScript in hours, restoring visualizations that had broken when browsers dropped Java support. He found only one minor bug across the ported code, while the agent surfaced two pre-existing bugs he had missed, and he also finally built a Minkowski-space relativity tool he had abandoned in 1999.

title: Migrating a production AI agent to GPT-5.6: 2.2x faster, 27% cheaper
blurb: Ploy migrated its production website-building agent from Claude Opus 4.8 to OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol, landing builds in under half the wall-clock time at 27% lower cost while scoring at or above the incumbent. Despite running on Vercel's provider-agnostic AI SDK, the switch surfaced one eval failure at a time that tool-argument filling, prompt caching, and reasoning replay are provider-specific behaviors the whole stack had quietly specialized around.

title: Inside a Kubernetes Bug Fix: Investigating an Integer Overflow in Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA)
blurb: A developer walks through fixing an integer overflow in Kubernetes' Dynamic Resource Allocation subsystem, where intermediate arithmetic during resource range alignment could overflow before producing a final result near math.MaxInt64. The proposed fix uses saturating arithmetic to clamp results to the 64-bit maximum instead of wrapping, keeping behavior deterministic, and adds edge-case tests exercising values near the integer limit to guard against regressions.

title: Don't you mean extinct?
blurb: Using Phil Tippett's "I feel extinct" moment from Jurassic Park's shift to CGI, the author argues that programmers fearing obsolescence from LLMs are facing the same recurring disruption every generation of developers has weathered, from the early web to mobile-first. The advice is to treat LLMs as another tool worth mastering rather than a threat, and the post points to Andrej Karpathy's 25-hour video series as the best resource for learning how they actually work.

title: Tiny Emulators
blurb: Tiny8bit is a collection of cycle-accurate 8-bit machine emulators compiled to WebAssembly, covering the C64, ZX Spectrum, Amstrad CPC, VIC-20, KC85, and Robotron systems directly in the browser. Each machine ships with a debugger UI and a library of playable games and demoscene productions, alongside Visual 6502 and Z80 remixes that trace signals at the transistor level.

title: Show HN: Mindwalk – Replay coding-agent sessions on a 3D map of your codebase
blurb: Mindwalk is a Go tool that replays coding-agent sessions as light moving across a 3D map of your repository, glowing where the agent searched, read, and edited so its understanding of the task becomes visible at a glance. A single local binary parses Claude Code and Codex session logs into terrain and timeline views with scrubable playback, touch states, and friction signals like error rate and edits after the last verify, with no session data leaving your machine.

title: AI coding tool users are flooding open-source projects with low-quality contributions, overwhelming maintainers and potentially eroding community engagement (Sam Learner/Financial Times)
blurb: Maintainers report that AI coding tools are generating a surge of low-effort pull requests, forcing reviewers to spend more time triaging plausible-looking but broken or unnecessary contributions than they save. The piece argues this triage burden risks pushing burned-out maintainers away and eroding the volunteer engagement that open source depends on.

title: Prefer strict tables in SQLite
blurb: SQLite's STRICT tables enforce rigid column typing, rejecting inserts and updates that store the wrong datatype, such as text in an INTEGER column, while still allowing lossless conversions like '123' to an integer. Appending STRICT to a table definition also blocks bogus column types like DATETIME, JSON, or UUID at creation time, catching typos and misconceptions about SQLite's supported datatypes.

title: Show HN: Learn by rebuilding Redis, Git, a database from scratch
blurb: ShipThatCode offers 80+ build-from-scratch courses that teach systems like Redis, Git, databases, and container runtimes by having you implement them in one of nine languages, with each lesson structured as choose, write, then run. The platform also bundles courses into sequenced career paths for roles including backend, frontend, DevOps, and data science, and the free tier requires no credit card.

title: A pure scheme web programming tool
blurb: Goeteia is a self-hosted Scheme-to-WebAssembly compiler that ships as a ~70 KB gzipped Wasm blob and recompiles source in the browser in about 15 ms per Run, with no server involved. It handles fixnums as unboxed i31refs, does every tail call as a return_call so a 100M-iteration loop runs in constant stack in ~150 ms, and includes a reactive web stack for DOM, Three.js, raw WebGL, and s-expression RPC.

title: Show HN: Shirei, cross-platform GUI framework in native Go
blurb: Shirei is a cross-platform GUI framework that lets developers build desktop apps in pure Go rather than HTML and JavaScript, producing native executables around 10MB that look identical across macOS, Windows, and Linux. Its immediate-mode API drops widget-state bookkeeping entirely, and it ships with complex text shaping, bidirectional layout, and IME support for East Asian languages out of the box.

title: Stop Hooks as Hard Constraints: Enforcing Claude Code Behavior Outside the Model
blurb: The author argues that prompt instructions are soft constraints that RLHF-trained patterns override under pressure, so behavioral rules should be enforced with Claude Code stop hooks that run after each response and can exit 2 to force a revision. To avoid false positives on legitimate clarifying questions, the post recommends an AND-gate pattern that fires only when a response matches an anti-pattern regex and the transcript shows prior data-collection tool calls.

title: Understanding the Odin programming language
blurb: Karl Zylinski's "Understanding the Odin Programming Language" teaches the language from procedures and manual memory management through parametric polymorphism and data-oriented design, aimed at anyone with prior programming experience. The book emphasizes why Odin makes its design choices rather than only how to write it, and version 1.10 swaps the deprecated Small_Array for the Fixed Capacity Dynamic Array.

title: Why Vanilla JavaScript
blurb: The author argues that building web apps in plain JavaScript, without a framework, avoids the churn of dependency upgrades, build tooling, and abstraction layers that outlive their usefulness. The case rests on longevity and control: code written against browser APIs directly keeps working for years, while the trade-off is writing more of the plumbing that React or Vue would otherwise hand you.

title: Ghostel.el: Terminal emulator powered by libghostty
blurb: Ghostel.el is an Emacs terminal emulator built on libghostty-vt, the same VT engine behind the Ghostty terminal, with a native Zig dynamic module handling terminal state, rendering, and PTY I/O while Elisp manages buffers, keymaps, and remote process integration. Following emacs-libvterm's two-layer design, it adds capabilities libvterm lacks, including the Kitty keyboard and graphics protocols, OSC 8 hyperlinks, rich underline styles, and synchronized output, with the native module downloaded automatically on first use.

title: Claude Code Burns 33K Tokens Before It Even Reads Your Prompt
blurb: A logging proxy placed between the model endpoint and two agentic coding CLIs found that Claude Code sends ~32,800 tokens of system prompt, tool schemas, and scaffolding before your prompt is read, versus ~6,900 for OpenCode. On identical benchmark tasks that both harnesses passed, Claude Code consumed 3.7x the input tokens, with 27 shipped tool schemas and shifting cache prefixes driving 53.7x more cache writes.

title: We scaled PgBouncer to 4x throughput
blurb: ClickHouse ran a fleet of PgBouncer processes bound to the same port with so_reuseport, letting the kernel load-balance connections across all cores instead of pinning connection pooling to a single one. On a 16-vCPU box, this pushed throughput from a single process's 87k transactions/sec peak to roughly 336k, about 4x. The hard part was query cancellation: cancel requests arrive on new connections the kernel can route to the wrong process, so the pooler processes peer with each other to forward cancels to the session's real owner.

title: Prefer strict tables in SQLite
blurb: SQLite's STRICT tables enforce rigid column typing, rejecting inserts and updates that put the wrong datatype in a column instead of silently coercing them the way default tables do.
